Walter Scott, 54. Football coach.
His team plays tomorrow morning.
He would rather not be here.

Abdominal Autonomics
Against Medical Advice

Walter Scott, 54. Football coach. His team plays tomorrow morning. He would rather not be here.

Walter is a 54-year-old football coach with no significant past medical history. He presents with central abdominal pain that began suddenly six hours ago. He describes it as a dull, colicky ache — difficult to pinpoint — spreading around his navel. He rates it 7/10. He has felt nauseated since the pain started. Nothing has relieved it. No urinary symptoms; last bowel motion was normal. When asked if there is anything else the team should know, he pauses — then says his team has a match tomorrow morning and he is the only coach. He has not volunteered this until now.

SOCRATES — Systematic History
Site: Central / periumbilical
Onset: Sudden, 6 hours ago
Character: Dull, colicky
Radiation: None yet
Associations: Nausea
Timing: Constant, worsening
Exacerbating: Movement
Severity: 7/10
Learning Objectives
Section 1
Interpret the character and location of abdominal pain using the principles of visceral and somatic innervation
Go to section →
Section 2
Trace the autonomic pathway from an abdominal organ to the spinal cord — efferent and afferent
Go to section →
Section 3
Apply anatomical reasoning to a real clinical decision
Go to section →
1
The Presentation
Walter has arrived. What does his pain character tell you?
LO 1 · Presentation
Walter's pain is central, dull and colicky, and difficult to localise. Which nerve fibre type is responsible for transmitting this kind of poorly localised visceral abdominal pain?
Poorly localised, dull, colicky abdominal pain is the hallmark of visceral pain, transmitted by unmyelinated C-fibres travelling as visceral afferents alongside the sympathetic efferents. These fibres signal that something is wrong within the abdomen, but cannot tell you precisely where. They are distinct from somatic Aδ fibres, which carry the sharp, well-localised pain of parietal peritoneal irritation. At this stage, we cannot yet identify Walter's organ. What we can say is that his pain is visceral in character.
LO 1 · Presentation
On examination, the intern finds tenderness at McBurney's point in the Right Iliac Fossa (RIF). The pain has also shifted from periumbilical to the RIF. What do these findings together indicate anatomically?
The combination of periumbilical-to-RIF pain migration and McBurney's point tenderness tells us two things anatomically.

First, the original central pain was visceral — C-fibres that could not localise beyond the midline. Second, the new RIF tenderness is somatic — the overlying parietal peritoneum is now involved, innervated by dermatomally precise somatic afferents via spinal nerves.

McBurney's point lies one-third of the way from the Anterior Superior Iliac Spine (ASIS) to the umbilicus. Its tenderness, combined with the pain migration pattern, now points toward a midgut structure in the Right Iliac Fossa. This is the evidence that begins to implicate the appendix.
✦
2
The Anatomy
Trace the pathway. Understand why pain appears where it does.
Visceral afferent pathway from the appendix. Unmyelinated C-fibres travel with the lesser splanchnic nerve, pass through — without synapsing in — the superior mesenteric ganglion, and synapse at the Dorsal Root Ganglion (DRG) at T10–T11. The brain maps this to the T10 dermatome, producing periumbilical referred pain.
T6 — Foregut (epigastric referred pain)
Foregut structures — stomach, proximal duodenum, liver, gallbladder, and pancreas — send visceral afferents via the greater splanchnic nerve, entering the spinal cord at T5–T9 (principally T6–T8). The brain maps this to the T6 dermatome: the epigastric region, between the xiphisternum and umbilicus. This is why peptic ulcer pain, biliary colic, and pancreatitis all present as central upper abdominal pain.
T10–T11 — Midgut (periumbilical referred pain)
Midgut structures — the appendix, small bowel, and proximal large bowel — send visceral afferents via the lesser splanchnic nerve, entering at T10–T11. The brain maps this to the periumbilical region. Walter's early pain is classic midgut visceral referred pain. The appendix is a midgut derivative, so pain always starts here before somatic localisation.
L1 — Somatic localisation to the Right Iliac Fossa
Once appendiceal inflammation reaches the parietal peritoneum, somatic afferents are activated. The parietal peritoneum of the Right Iliac Fossa is innervated by the iliohypogastric and ilioinguinal nerves (L1) — dermatomally precise. Pain is now sharply localised to the RIF. This transition from periumbilical to RIF pain marks the shift from visceral to somatic involvement — a critical clinical sign in appendicitis.
LO 2 · Pathway
Trace the sympathetic efferent pathway from spinal cord to appendix. Select the correct structure for each step.
Spinal cord origin
↓
Pre-ganglionic nerve
↓
Pre-vertebral ganglion
↓
Target organ
1. IMLCC (T10–T11) — Preganglionic cell bodies in the lateral horn of the spinal cord.
2. Lesser splanchnic nerve — Preganglionic fibres exit via white rami communicantes and bypass the paravertebral chain.
3. Superior mesenteric ganglion — Synapse occurs here. Midgut structures synapse at the SMG, not the coeliac ganglion.
4. Appendix via post-ganglionic fibres — Unmyelinated post-ganglionic fibres travel along blood vessels to the appendix wall.
LO 2 · Pathway
Now consider how the same principles apply beyond Walter's case. A different patient presents with colicky pain in the epigastric region — between the xiphisternum and umbilicus. No Right Iliac Fossa tenderness. Which visceral afferent level and gut region best explains this?
T6 → epigastric referred pain. Foregut structures — stomach, proximal duodenum, gallbladder, liver, and pancreas — send visceral afferents via the greater splanchnic nerve to the spinal cord at T5–T9 (principally T6–T8). The brain maps this to the epigastric region. Peptic ulcer disease, biliary colic, and pancreatitis all present here.

Contrast with Walter's periumbilical pain, which mapped to T10–T11 via the lesser splanchnic nerve — indicating a midgut structure. The anatomical level of visceral afferent entry is the key to localising the organ of origin.
LO 2 · Pathway
Complete the autonomic pathway for each gut division — spinal level, nerve, ganglion, and organs.
Select the correct answer for each blank. Each row has one correct answer per column.
Foregut
Midgut
Hindgut
Spinal level
↓
↓
↓
Splanchnic nerve
↓
↓
↓
Ganglion
↓
↓
↓
Organs
Foregut (T5–T9): Greater splanchnic nerve → Coeliac ganglion → Stomach, liver, gallbladder, pancreas. Referred pain: epigastric.
Midgut (T10–T11): Lesser splanchnic nerve → Superior mesenteric ganglion → Appendix, small bowel, proximal colon. Referred pain: periumbilical.
Hindgut (T12–L1): Lumbar splanchnic nerves → Inferior mesenteric ganglion → Distal colon, sigmoid, rectum. Referred pain: hypogastric.
LO 2 · Pathway
True or false: Visceral afferents from the appendix synapse in the superior mesenteric ganglion before entering the spinal cord at T10–T11.
False. Visceral afferents are sensory fibres — they are not part of the two-neuron efferent chain and do not synapse in prevertebral ganglia. They travel alongside the sympathetic efferents through the lesser splanchnic nerve and pass through the superior mesenteric ganglion without synapsing. They enter the spinal cord and synapse at the Dorsal Root Ganglion (DRG) at T10–T11. The ganglion synapse belongs exclusively to the efferent pathway — as shown in the visceral afferent pathway diagram above.
LO 2 · Pathway
The senior asks: "Walter's pain started central. Why not to the side?" Sort each organ — does its visceral pain refer to the midline, or can it lateralise?
Not all visceral pain refers to the midline. Think about whether each organ is paired or unpaired.
Appendix
Stomach
Kidney
Ovary
Small bowel
Testis
Midline referred pain →
Can lateralise →
Midline referred pain — unpaired organs (bilateral innervation)
The appendix, stomach, and small bowel are unpaired midline structures. Their visceral afferents enter the spinal cord bilaterally — symmetrically on both sides. The brain cannot determine laterality from a bilateral signal, so pain is referred to the midline. This is why Walter's appendicitis started as central, periumbilical pain.
Can lateralise — paired organs (predominantly unilateral innervation)
The kidneys, ovaries, and testes are paired lateral structures. Each organ's visceral afferents enter the spinal cord predominantly ipsilaterally. The brain receives a one-sided signal and localises it — which is why renal colic lateralises to the affected flank, and ovarian or testicular pain lateralises to the affected side.
Clinical rule: Midline visceral pain = unpaired organ. Lateralised visceral pain = consider a paired structure.
LO 2 · Pathway
A 28-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset right-sided lower abdominal pain that does not migrate to the midline. Which is most anatomically consistent?
Right-sided pain that does not migrate to the midline suggests a paired organ with predominantly unilateral innervation. The right ovary sends visceral afferents ipsilaterally — the brain correctly lateralises this to the right. Early appendicitis would produce central periumbilical pain (bilateral innervation of an unpaired midgut structure) before any somatic Right Iliac Fossa localisation. This distinction — midline versus lateralised visceral pain — is a key clinical reasoning tool in acute abdomen assessment.
✦
3
The AMA Decision
Walter wants to leave. Defend your decision anatomically.
LO 3 · Decision
"I feel better. I want to go home." After IV analgesia, Walter's pain drops to 3/10. Which statement best explains this anatomically?
IV opioid analgesia acts centrally — at μ-opioid receptors in the dorsal horn and supraspinal centres — to modulate pain perception. It does not reduce peripheral nociceptive input. The appendix remains inflamed; visceral C-fibres continue to fire, transmitting signals via the lesser splanchnic nerve, through the superior mesenteric ganglion, to the Dorsal Root Ganglion (DRG) at T10–T11. A lower pain score reflects altered central processing — not resolution of pathology. Analgesia masks perception, not pathology.
LO 3 · Decision
Sort each clinical finding — does it support admission, or could it be used to argue for discharge?
McBurney's point tenderness
Pain improved with analgesia
Periumbilical → Right Iliac Fossa pain migration
Associated nausea
Pain now 3/10
6-hour worsening history
Admit →
Discharge argument →
Support admission: McBurney's point tenderness indicates parietal peritoneal irritation — a somatic sign of advancing inflammation. Periumbilical-to-Right Iliac Fossa pain migration is the classic anatomical progression of appendicitis (visceral C-fibre → somatic Aδ activation). Nausea and 6-hour worsening history are consistent with evolving pathology.
Discharge argument — but anatomically insufficient: Pain improvement and a lower score reflect central modulation of perception only — not resolution of inflammation. Visceral afferents continue to fire. These findings alone cannot justify discharge when somatic signs are present.
